content.body: Developmental biology studies how organisms grow from a single cell. Stem cells are undifferentiated cells that can become specialised. This field has major medical applications.

Fertilisation → Zygote → Cleavage → Morula → Blastula → Gastrula → Organogenesis

Stem cell types:
- Totipotent: can form entire organism
- Pluripotent: can form all body tissues (embryonic stem cells)
- Multipotent: limited range (adult stem cells)

content.title: Embryonic Layers
content.body:
Gastrulation forms 3 primary germ layers:
Ectoderm → skin, nervous system
Mesoderm → muscles, bones, blood, kidneys
Endoderm → digestive tract, lungs, liver
All organs derive from these three layers

questions.question: The zygote is formed by:
questions.answer: Fertilisation
questions.explanation: The zygote is the single cell formed when sperm fuses with egg

questions.question: Pluripotent stem cells can differentiate into:
questions.answer: All body tissues
questions.explanation: Pluripotent cells can form all cell types in the body (but not placenta)

questions.question: The nervous system develops from which germ layer?
questions.answer: Ectoderm
questions.explanation: The ectoderm gives rise to the skin and entire nervous system

questions.question: Gastrulation produces:
questions.answer: Three germ layers
questions.explanation: Gastrulation establishes the three primary germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm

questions.question: Adult stem cells are:
questions.answer: Multipotent
questions.explanation: Adult stem cells are multipotent — they can differentiate into a limited range of related cell types

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

content.body: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells capable of self-renewal and differentiation into specialised cell types. They hold great promise for regenerative medicine.

content.title: Types of Stem Cells
content.body:
Totipotent: can form entire organism including placenta
(fertilised egg up to 8-cell stage)

Pluripotent: can form all body cells but not placenta
(embryonic stem cells — inner cell mass of blastocyst)

Multipotent: can form several related cell types
(adult stem cells — bone marrow haematopoietic stem cells → all blood cells)

Unipotent: can form only one cell type
(muscle satellite cells → muscle fibres)

Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs):
Adult somatic cells reprogrammed to pluripotent state by introducing transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc)
Ethical advantage over embryonic stem cells
Used for disease modelling, drug testing, potential therapy

Stem cell therapies:
Bone marrow transplant (leukaemia treatment) — established
Corneal grafts — clinical use
Cardiac repair — experimental

questions.question: Totipotent stem cells can...
options: Form all body cells but not placenta
Form the complete organism including placenta
questions.answer: Form the complete organism including placenta
questions.explanation: Totipotent cells (early embryo) can form every cell type including extraembryonic tissues like the placenta.

questions.question: Embryonic stem cells are...
questions.answer: Pluripotent
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells from the inner cell mass are pluripotent — they can form all cells of the body but not extraembryonic tissues.

questions.question: iPSCs (induced pluripotent stem cells) are made by...
options: Reprogramming adult cells with transcription factors
questions.answer: Reprogramming adult cells with transcription factors
questions.explanation: Yamanaka (2006) showed that introducing 4 transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc) into adult cells reprograms them to pluripotency.

questions.question: An advantage of iPSCs over embryonic stem cells is...
options: They avoid ethical issues of destroying embryos
questions.answer: They avoid ethical issues of destroying embryos
questions.explanation: iPSCs are made from adult cells, avoiding the ethical controversy of destroying human embryos for embryonic stem cells.

questions.question: Haematopoietic stem cells are...
questions.answer: Multipotent (form all blood cells)
questions.explanation: Haematopoietic (blood) stem cells in bone marrow are multipotent — they can form all types of blood cells.

questions.question: Bone marrow transplant is used to treat...
questions.answer: Leukaemia
questions.explanation: Bone marrow transplant replaces cancerous or destroyed blood-forming cells with healthy haematopoietic stem cells.

questions.question: Cell differentiation involves...
questions.answer: Selective gene expression
questions.explanation: Differentiation: cells have the same DNA but express different genes. Muscle cells express muscle-specific genes; neurons express neural genes.

questions.question: The niche of a stem cell is...
options: Its location in a tissue that maintains stem cell properties
questions.answer: Its location in a tissue that maintains stem cell properties
questions.explanation: The stem cell niche is the microenvironment (surrounding cells, ECM, signals) that maintains stem cell identity and controls self-renewal.

questions.question: Therapeutic cloning involves...
options: Creating embryos for stem cells with patient's DNA to avoid rejection
questions.answer: Creating embryos for stem cells with patient's DNA to avoid rejection
questions.explanation: Therapeutic cloning: nucleus from patient's cell placed in enucleated egg → embryo → stem cells with patient's genotype → autologous transplant.

questions.question: Which property distinguishes stem cells from normal differentiated cells?
questions.answer: Self-renewal and ability to differentiate
questions.explanation: Stem cells can divide indefinitely (self-renewal) and produce specialised daughter cells (differentiation) — properties lost by most differentiated cells.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Stem cells: undifferentiated cells that can divide and differentiate
Totipotent: can form any cell type (fertilised egg up to 8-cell stage)
Pluripotent: can form any body cell (embryonic stem cells)
Multipotent: restricted to specific lineages (adult stem cells)

Stem cell therapies:
Leukaemia: bone marrow transplant
Burns: skin grafts from stem cells
Parkinson's: dopamine neuron replacement
Diabetes: pancreatic beta cell replacement

content.title: Cloning Methods
content.body:
Reproductive cloning:
Somatic Cell Nuclear Transfer (SCNT): nucleus from somatic cell into enucleated egg
Dolly sheep (1996): first mammal cloned this way

Therapeutic cloning: SCNT but embryo used for stem cells, not implanted
Individualised medicine: patient's own cells -> no immune rejection

Ethical issues:
- Embryo destruction
- Reproductive cloning of humans (banned)
- Genetic diversity reduction

content.title: Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)
content.body:
Yamanaka (2006, Nobel Prize 2012):
Add 4 transcription factors to adult somatic cell -> reprogrammed to pluripotent state
Advantages: patient's own cells, no ethical issues with embryos
Challenges: risk of tumour formation, efficiency low

Organoids: 3D mini-organs grown from stem cells for drug testing

questions.question: Totipotent stem cells can form
options: any cell type including placenta - found in very early embryo
questions.answer: any cell type including placenta - found in very early embryo
questions.explanation: Totipotent: total potential. Zygote and cells up to 8-cell stage. Can form embryo AND extra-embryonic tissues (placenta)

questions.question: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent meaning
options: can form any of the ~200 body cell types but NOT placenta
questions.answer: can form any of the ~200 body cell types but NOT placenta
questions.explanation: Pluripotent: plural potential. ESCs from inner cell mass. Can become any somatic cell type but not trophoblast/placenta

questions.question: Dolly the sheep demonstrated
options: adult cell nucleus can be reprogrammed to totipotent state (SCNT cloning)
questions.answer: adult cell nucleus can be reprogrammed to totipotent state (SCNT cloning)
questions.explanation: SCNT: Dolly's nucleus from mammary gland cell placed in enucleated egg. Showed differentiation is epigenetically reversible. First mammal cloned from adult cell

questions.question: Therapeutic cloning differs from reproductive cloning in that
options: embryo used to harvest stem cells (not implanted) for patient-specific therapies
questions.answer: embryo used to harvest stem cells (not implanted) for patient-specific therapies
questions.explanation: Therapeutic: SCNT creates embryo -> extract ESCs for patient-specific therapy -> no immune rejection. NOT implanted for birth

questions.question: iPSCs (induced pluripotent stem cells) are advantageous because
options: patient's own adult cells reprogrammed -> personalised, no embryo destroyed, no immune rejection
questions.answer: patient's own adult cells reprogrammed -> personalised, no embryo destroyed, no immune rejection
questions.explanation: Yamanaka's iPSCs: adult cells + 4 transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc) -> pluripotent. Ethical advantage over ESCs

questions.question: Bone marrow transplant is a form of stem cell therapy that treats
options: leukaemia by replacing patient's blood-forming stem cells with healthy donor cells
questions.answer: leukaemia by replacing patient's blood-forming stem cells with healthy donor cells
questions.explanation: Haematopoietic stem cell transplant: patient's cancerous bone marrow destroyed by chemo/radiation then replaced with donor HSCs that rebuild healthy blood system

questions.question: Main ethical concern about embryonic stem cell research is
options: destruction of human embryo to obtain cells
questions.answer: destruction of human embryo to obtain cells
questions.explanation: ESC extraction destroys the blastocyst (5-7 day embryo). Some consider this equivalent to ending human life. Major ethical debate globally

questions.question: Organoids grown from stem cells are used for
options: testing drugs on 3D mini-organs that better mimic real organ function than flat cell cultures
questions.answer: testing drugs on 3D mini-organs that better mimic real organ function than flat cell cultures
questions.explanation: Organoids: self-organising 3D structures from stem cells (mini-brain, mini-gut, mini-kidney). More physiologically relevant than 2D culture for drug testing

questions.question: Multipotent stem cells differ from pluripotent because
options: they are restricted to producing cells of a specific tissue type (e.g. haematopoietic -> blood only)
questions.answer: they are restricted to producing cells of a specific tissue type (e.g. haematopoietic -> blood only)
questions.explanation: Multipotent: many potential but limited. Adult stem cells in tissues produce only that tissue type. Less flexible than pluripotent ESCs

questions.question: Reproductive cloning of humans is internationally banned primarily because
options: ethical concerns about identity, uniqueness, exploitation, unknown health risks, and treating humans as products
questions.answer: ethical concerns about identity, uniqueness, exploitation, unknown health risks, and treating humans as products
questions.explanation: Human reproductive cloning: banned by most countries. Concerns: human dignity, genetic determinism, high failure rate (Dolly had early ageing), social implications

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.answer: Mitochondria

questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Developmental Biology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Developmental Biology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Developmental Biology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Developmental Biology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Developmental Biology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Developmental Biology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Developmental Biology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Developmental Biology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Developmental Biology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Developmental Biology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Developmental Biology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Developmental Biology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Developmental Biology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Developmental Biology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
